metadata.subject: Science
metadata.description: Chemistry 12 is the capstone senior chemistry course, extending students' understanding into reaction kinetics, dynamic equilibrium, solubility equilibrium, acid-base chemistry, and electrochemistry. Students engage in sophisticated quantitative problem-solving, advanced laboratory work, and critical analysis of chemical applications in society and the environment. The course prepares students for post-secondary chemistry and related disciplines.
metadata.general_description: An advanced senior chemistry course covering reaction rates and kinetics, dynamic equilibrium, solubility, acid-base chemistry, and electrochemistry — developing sophisticated quantitative reasoning and advanced laboratory skills for post-secondary pathways.

big_ideas.statement: Reactants must collide to react, and the reaction rate is dependent on the surrounding conditions.
big_ideas.elaboration: Collision theory explains how the rate of a chemical reaction depends on the frequency and energy of molecular collisions — affected by concentration, temperature, surface area, and catalysts. Reaction mechanisms describe the step-by-step pathways through which reactions proceed.

big_ideas.statement: Dynamic equilibrium can be shifted by changes to the surrounding conditions.
big_ideas.elaboration: In a dynamic equilibrium, forward and reverse reactions occur at equal rates. Le Châtelier's principle describes how changes in concentration, temperature, or pressure shift this balance — a concept central to industrial chemistry and biological systems.

big_ideas.statement: Saturated solutions are systems in equilibrium.
big_ideas.elaboration: When a solution is saturated, the rate of dissolution equals the rate of crystallization — a dynamic equilibrium described quantitatively by the solubility product constant (Ksp). This framework explains precipitation reactions and has applications from water treatment to kidney stones.

big_ideas.statement: Acid or base strength depends on the degree of ion dissociation.
big_ideas.elaboration: Acids and bases are defined by their tendency to donate or accept protons (Brønsted-Lowry). Strong acids and bases fully dissociate; weak ones partially dissociate — a distinction with significant quantitative and practical consequences, from pH calculations to biological buffering.

big_ideas.statement: Oxidation and reduction are complementary processes that involve the gain or loss of electrons.
big_ideas.elaboration: In redox reactions, electrons transferred from reducing agents to oxidizing agents drive chemical change — the foundation of electrochemical cells (batteries), electrolysis, and industrial processes such as metal refining and electroplating.

competencies.raw: Sustain intellectual curiosity; make observations to identify questions; formulate multiple hypotheses and predict outcomes.
i_can_statements: I can identify complex questions about chemical kinetics, equilibrium, acid-base chemistry, and electrochemistry.
I can formulate specific, testable hypotheses for advanced chemistry investigations.
I can predict quantitative outcomes — concentrations, rates, pH — for chemical systems at equilibrium.

competencies.raw: Collaboratively plan investigations; assess risks; use SI units and equipment; apply accuracy and precision concepts.
i_can_statements: I can design advanced chemistry investigations with appropriate controls and quantitative methods.
I can handle hazardous chemicals safely and identify risks in advanced laboratory procedures.
I can collect precise quantitative data using burettes, pH meters, spectrometers, and electrochemical equipment.
I can apply significant figures and error analysis to advanced chemistry calculations.

categories.name: Processing and Analyzing Data and Information
categories.raw: Processing and Analyzing Data and Information

competencies.raw: Interpret environments; apply First Peoples perspectives; seek patterns in data; construct and analyze graphs and models; draw evidence-based conclusions.
i_can_statements: I can apply First Peoples perspectives on chemical processes in nature (e.g., fermentation, acid-base in ecosystems).
I can identify patterns in kinetics data and use them to determine reaction order and rate law.
I can construct and interpret equilibrium expression (K) and reaction quotient (Q) calculations.
I can draw conclusions about equilibrium positions, pH, and electrochemical potentials from quantitative data.

competencies.raw: Assess methods and identify errors; improve investigations; evaluate model validity; exercise informed skepticism; analyze ethical implications.
i_can_statements: I can identify sources of systematic and random error in advanced chemistry experiments.
I can evaluate the validity and limitations of chemical equilibrium and kinetics models.
I can exercise informed skepticism about chemical claims in industrial, medical, and environmental contexts.
I can analyze the ethical and environmental implications of industrial chemical processes.

competencies.raw: Design projects with real-world applications; solve problems collaboratively; implement multiple strategies.
i_can_statements: I can apply kinetics, equilibrium, and electrochemistry concepts to solve real-world engineering and industrial problems.
I can design a chemistry solution or innovation addressing a health, environmental, or industrial challenge.
I can implement multiple problem-solving strategies including ICE tables, Nernst equation, and Hess's Law.

competencies.raw: Create theoretical models; construct evidence-based arguments using scientific language and conventions.
i_can_statements: I can construct and explain theoretical models for reaction mechanisms, equilibrium systems, and electrochemical cells.
I can communicate advanced chemistry concepts using correct IUPAC notation and conventions.
I can write laboratory reports presenting complex quantitative results and evidence-based analysis.

areas.name: Reaction Kinetics
areas.raw: Reactants must collide to react; reaction rate depends on surrounding conditions.
topics: Collision theory: effective collisions, activation energy, orientation
Factors affecting reaction rate: concentration, temperature, surface area, catalysts
Rate laws: rate = k[A]^m[B]^n — determining rate order experimentally
Reaction mechanisms: elementary steps, rate-determining step, intermediates
Energy diagrams: activation energy, enthalpy change, effect of catalysts
Biological catalysts: enzymes as biochemical catalysts

areas.name: Dynamic Equilibrium
areas.raw: Dynamic equilibrium shifted by changes to surrounding conditions.
topics: Concept of dynamic equilibrium: forward and reverse reactions at equal rates
Equilibrium constant expression: K (Kc and Kp)
Reaction quotient (Q): determining direction of shift
ICE tables: calculating equilibrium concentrations
Le Châtelier's principle: effect of concentration, temperature, and pressure changes
Industrial applications: Haber process (ammonia), Contact process (sulfuric acid)

areas.name: Solubility Equilibrium
areas.raw: Saturated solutions as systems in equilibrium.
topics: Solubility product constant (Ksp): writing and calculating
Predicting precipitation: comparing Q to Ksp
Common ion effect: suppression of solubility
Applications: water treatment, kidney stones, qualitative ion analysis

areas.name: Acid-Base Chemistry
areas.raw: Acid or base strength depends on degree of ion dissociation.
topics: Brønsted-Lowry theory: acids as proton donors, bases as proton acceptors
Strong vs. weak acids and bases: full vs. partial dissociation
Acid and base dissociation constants: Ka and Kb
pH calculations: strong acid/base, weak acid/base, and buffer solutions
Hydrolysis of salts: acidic, basic, or neutral salt solutions
Titration: equivalence point, indicators, and titration curves
Buffer solutions: Henderson-Hasselbalch equation and biological buffering

areas.name: Electrochemistry
areas.raw: Oxidation and reduction as complementary processes involving electron transfer.
topics: Oxidation states: assigning and balancing redox equations
Half-reactions: oxidation and reduction half-reactions
Galvanic (voltaic) cells: structure, standard cell potential (E°), and the activity series
Standard reduction potentials and the electrochemical series
Spontaneity: ΔG = -nFE relationship
Electrolytic cells: electrolysis, Faraday's laws, and applications
Industrial applications: electroplating, metal refining, batteries, and hydrogen fuel cells
